keyword.string: Web XHTML list unordered ordered bullets

item.value: How to display a list of items on a web page
Created by Wimba Create, licenced to South Central College

para.b: pic001.jpgpic002.gifPeople don't read web sites, they scan them.

newpage.para: pic003.jpgUsing lists is an excellent way to display lots of information in a manner that can be easily scanned and understood.
With a little CSS it is also easy to convert a list into an effective menu system.

courseGenie.newpage:
Unordered List
Start with a standard XHTML file using your favorite text editor. pic004.jpg The
element is used to frame the items to be listed. Each item is designated using the
    • element. Normally the
    • element is kept on the same line as the text. Each
  • element will display a bullet (solid round circle) next to the text of the item: pic005.jpg Notice the comment on line #15 that describes the contents of the list. pic006.jpg Also, for readability, indent the list of items using three spaces. (Don't use the TAB key because TABS change size on different computer systems.) If you are using NotePad++ you can set the preferences (settings/preferences/Edit Components tab) so all TABS will automatically be converted to three spaces. Here is a screen shot of the preference screen in NotePad++: pic007.jpg

para.b: An ordered list uses similar elements except that the outer element is <ol> for ordered list.
Type in this code below the unordered list:

para.longdesc: Full source code file included at end of the tutorial.

para.b: Here's what the output should look like:

image.alt: Output showing unordered (bulleted) list and ordered list (numbers).

para.longdesc: Full source code file included at end of the tutorial.

para.b: Don't forget the stop tag for each start tag!
Changes not showing up in the browser?

list.item: Make sure you are saving the file each time you make a change (CTRL S).

Make sure you are viewing the same file you are editing.
Add some weird but noticeable characters to the code such as XYZ.
Save the code and view the file in the browser.
If the XYZ does not show up than you are probably looking at a different file.

newpage.para: You can easily change the display of the list by using the type=" " attribute. This can be used for either element: <ul>, <ol>, or <li>.
For the <ul> you can use type="disc", type="square", or type="circle".
For the <ol> you can use type="1", type="a", type="A", type="i", or type="I"
For the <li> you can use any of these.

para.b: Note that the value of every attribute must be enclosed inside of quotes.

newpage.para: Here are some examples. Type them in to see how the list changes:

image.alt: Using the type attribute to change the marker.

para.longdesc: Full source code file included at end of the tutorial.

newpage.para: Here is the output from this XHTML code:

image.alt: Output showing the results of different type attributes

newpage.heading: Debugging Tip

para.b: Does upper/lower case matter for the attribute values?

newpage.para: Try type="A" and type="a" to see.

para.b: Does spelling matter?

newpage.para: Try type="disk" or type="sq"

courseGenie.newpage:
Lists Inside of Lists Lists can be put inside of lists. Simply enclose the sublist inside one of the <li> of the parent list. This is a great way to put the type=" " attribute to work.
Here is some code you can type in to try this out.
pic012.jpg
And here is the output from this code:
pic013.jpg
Debug Tip
Use the HTML Validator FireFox addon. This will show you any validation errors or warnings that you may have with your code. If you don't have any errors than you know your code will display in all standard browsers, from cell phones to desktop browsers.
Here is an example of the HTML Validator at work.
If you don't put the sublist in the correct place you will get validation errors that display in the lower right corner of the screen.
pic014.jpg
If you double click on the information that look like this:
pic015.jpg
Notice how the sublist in the error code is outside of <li> pair. It will confuse most browsers causing the lists to possibly display incorrectly or not at all.
It is much more efficient to fix errors as they happen than later, after the web page is all finished.

para.b: With a little CSS (Cascading Style Sheet) magic you can add your own custom bullets.

newpage.para: (1) Save this bullet image by right-mouse clicking on it and selecting "save image as" from the option list: pic016.gif

para.b: (2) Save the file in the same folder as your web page.
(3) Type in the following code in the <head> section of the page:

image.alt: The style code for a custom bullet

para.b: When you display the page, all of the unordered lists (<ul>) should look like this:

image.alt: output showing the customized bullet for the unordered lists.

newpage.heading: Here's how CSS works

image.alt: The selector, property, and value for each CSS rule.

newpage.para: When the browser reads in a web page it stores the information in the information in the <head> element in memory.

para.b: The <style> element designates CSS, or how the page should be styled.

newpage.para: Each rule has the three parts:
selector tells the browser which element in the <body> of the page to style.
property describes what attribute of the element should be changed.
value what should be used to change the appearance of the element.
In this example the code is changing the list-style-image property of the ul element so that it displays the file named bullet.png.
Just like a sentence in English follows specific punctuation rules, each selector uses a set of curly braces { } to "frame" the list of properties associated with it.
Each property ends with a colon ":" separating it from the value.
Each value ends with a semicolon ";" designating the end of the line.
The url( ) tells the browser to look in the file system for the file that is designated.
Commenting is different inside the <style> element. You have to start the comment using /* and end it using */
Be generous with your comments, especially on special items (such as color names). The person you help in the future may be yourself!
newpage.heading: Debugging Tip

para.b: If the custom bullets do not appear in your browser go through this checklist:

list.item: Did you spell everything correctly? (particularly the name of the bullet graphic).
Do you have the colons after the property and the semicolons at the end of the line?
Are the curly braces in the correct places: one after the selector and one at the end of the entire rule, boxing in the property and its value.
Is the bullet graphic file in the same folder as the web page?

para.b: Want to display your lists using Japanese?

newpage.para: Try this CSS property for the ordered list:

para.b: Here is what is will look like:

newpage.para: Here is a copy of the complete source code so you can see the entire page and how each part relates to the others.
list.htmlmedia/list.html - right-mouse click, select "save link as"

para.b: At this point you should be able to do the following on any web page you write:

list.item: Create an ordered list that is numbered, lettered, or uses Roman Numerals
Create a list inside of a list using valid XHTML coding.
Use CSS to use custom images for bullets
Have a debugging checklist in your head that will help you correct some of the most common problems with lists and CSS.
Have a basic understanding of the three parts and punctuation of a CSS selector.

para.b: This completes the tutorial: Web Development - Lists

newpage.para: May 2009
Written by Peter K. Johnson, Instructor
South Central College
North Mankato, MN 56003
